context: The Word of the Lord came unto Jonah the son of Amittai.
point_summary: God's direct word and call to Jonah ("the Word of the Lord came unto him") establishes him as a genuine prophet who was commissioned as a missionary to Nineveh yet chose disobedient flight.

context: Now the Lord came unto Jonah the son of Amittai saying, Arise and go to Nineveh that great city.
point_summary: God's sovereign call to Jonah to preach against Nineveh's wickedness sets up the central tension: Jonah's hatred of the Ninevites versus God's love and desire to extend mercy to them.

context: Arise and go to Nineveh that great city. Now notice what he says and cry against it.
point_summary: God's command to preach against Nineveh's wickedness highlights the tension between Jonah's hatred for his enemies and God's love and desire to extend mercy even to the most vile.

context: But Jonah rose up to flee unto Tarshish from the presence of the Lord, from the presence of the Lord.
point_summary: Jonah's flight to Tarshish illustrates the tension between human hatred for the wicked and God's love for them, as God commissioned Jonah precisely to bring mercy to a people Jonah despised.

context: But Jonah rose up to flee, to run unto Tarshish from the presence of the Lord. And he went down to Joppa, and he found a ship going to Tarshish, so he paid the fare thereof, and he went down into it to go with him into Tarshish from the presence of the Lord.
point_summary: Jonah's deliberate flight "from the presence of the Lord" illustrates that disobedience to God's call forfeits the blessing and risks God raising up someone else to fulfill the mission.

context: wherefore they cried unto the Lord and said, we beseech thee, O Lord, we beseech thee, let us not perish for this man's life and lay not upon us innocent blood for thou, O Lord, hast done as it please thee.
point_summary: Jonah's disobedience unwittingly became a witness: the terrified sailors abandoned their false gods and cried out to the Lord, leading the preacher to conclude they were genuinely converted.

context: Then the men feared the Lord exceedingly and offered a sacrifice unto the Lord and made vows.
point_summary: The sailors' sacrifice and vows in verse 16 are read as evidence of genuine conversion—these pagan men placing faith in Jonah's God despite Jonah's disobedience, pointing to Christ's blood as the only sacrifice that saves from sin.

context: Now the Lord had prepared a great fish to swallow up Jonah.
point_summary: Jonah's three days and three nights in the fish's belly is identified as a messianic type, fulfilled in Matthew 12:40 by Christ's burial and resurrection.

context: Then Jonah prayed unto the Lord his God out of the fish's belly.
point_summary: Even in the depths of divine discipline, Jonah's prayer from the fish's belly demonstrates that God hears the cry of the afflicted and preserves life from corruption.

context: I cried by reason of mine affliction unto the Lord and he heard me out of the belly of hell, Sheol, and of course that can reference the grave as well, cried I and thou heardest my voice.
point_summary: Even in the depths of divine discipline, Jonah's cry reached God—demonstrating that prayer from the pit of affliction is heard and answered by the Lord.

context: For thou hast cast me into the deep and the midst of the seas and the floods come past me about and all the billows and thy waves passed over me.
point_summary: Jonah's description of being cast into the deep, overwhelmed by waves, and brought up from corruption illustrates God's sovereign preservation of him despite his own disobedience.

context: Then I said, I'm cast out of thy sight yet I will look again toward the holy temple.
point_summary: Even in the depths of despair and divine discipline, Jonah's declaration "yet I will look again toward the holy temple" expresses a defiant, persevering faith that refuses to abandon hope in God.

context: The waters can pass me about even to the soul.
point_summary: Jonah's vivid description of drowning—waters engulfing his soul, depths closing around him, seaweed at his head—illustrates the desperate extremity from which God sovereignly rescued him despite his own disobedience.

context: The depth closed me round about, the weeds were wrapped about my head.
point_summary: Even submerged to the soul and entangled in seaweed at the ocean's depths, Jonah survived three days in the fish solely because God's hand preserved him in spite of his own rebellion.

context: I went down to the bottoms of the mountains, the earth with her bars was about me forever yet hast thou brought up my life from corruption, O Lord my God.
point_summary: Even at the bottom of the mountains, with earth's bars closing around him forever, God still brought Jonah's life up from corruption—rescued by divine preservation despite his own disobedience.

context: When my soul fainted within me, I remembered the Lord and my prayer came in unto thee into thy holy temple.
point_summary: When the soul faints in extremity and remembers the Lord, prayer reaches God even from the depths—Jonah's cry from Sheol illustrating that divine rescue comes to the disobedient through hardship rather than willing obedience.

context: They that observe lying vanities forsake their own mercy
point_summary: Those who cling to lying vanities—self-reliance or rebellion—forfeit the mercy God stands ready to give, as Jonah's hard path through the fish's belly illustrates.

context: but I will sacrifice unto thee with the voice of thanksgiving, I will pay that thy vowed salvations of the Lord.
point_summary: Jonah's survival in the fish's belly—despite biological impossibility—demonstrated that God's preserving hand was upon him even in the midst of his disobedience, bringing him to the place of surrender expressed in 2:9.

context: And the Lord spake unto the fish and it vomited out Jonah upon the dry land.
point_summary: God's sovereign preservation of Jonah inside the fish for three days—a biological impossibility—demonstrates that divine purpose overrides natural limits even when the prophet arrived there through disobedience.

context: and the word of the Lord came unto Jonah the second time saying, Arise and go to Nineveh, that great city and preach unto it the preaching that I bid thee.
point_summary: God's recommissioning of Jonah with the same call he had fled illustrates that disobedience only delays the mission and brings unnecessary hardship when simple obedience would have sufficed.

context: and the word of the Lord came unto Jonah the second time saying, Arise and go to Nineveh, that great city and preach unto it the preaching that I bid thee.
point_summary: God's recommissioning of Jonah with the same command illustrates that obedience from the start would have spared the prophet the hard way, since the message and mission never changed.

context: So Jonah rose and went into Nineveh according to the word of the Lord.
point_summary: Jonah's obedience in 3:3 came only after severe discipline, illustrating that God's servants can reach the right destination by the hard way rather than simply walking in willing obedience from the start.

context: Now, Nineveh was an exceeding great city of three days journey. He took that long walk across that city and Jonah began to enter that city a day's journey and he cried.
point_summary: Jonah's eight-word message to Nineveh illustrates that obedience to God's call—however brief or reluctant—is far better than the costly detour of fleeing His will.

context: yet 40 days and Nineveh shall be overthrown
point_summary: Jonah's eight-word message of judgment, however reluctantly delivered, was exactly what God commissioned him to say, and simple obedience to that call would have spared him every hardship along the way.

context: So the people of Nineveh believed God. They believed him and they proclaimed it fast. They put on sackcloth from the greatest of them even to the least of them.
point_summary: Nineveh's city-wide repentance—from the least to the king himself—demonstrates that God accomplished His redemptive purpose through an unwilling messenger who simply delivered the word he was given.

context: For the word came unto the king of Nineveh and he rose from his throne and he laid his robe from him and he covered him with sackcloth and sat in ashes
point_summary: Jonah's costly disobedience is contrasted with what could have been willing, joyful obedience, illustrating that God accomplishes His purposes even through unwilling servants who resist His call.

context: he caused it to be proclaimed and published through Nineveh by the decree of the king and his nobles saying, let neither man nor beast, herd nor flock taste anything. Let them not feed nor drink water
point_summary: Jonah's disobedience made his path to Nineveh needlessly harrowing, whereas simple obedience from the start would have brought him to witness the same extraordinary, city-wide repentance without the suffering.

context: but let man and beast be covered with sackcloth and cry mightily unto God. Yea, let them turn, everyone from his evil way and from the violence that is in their hands
point_summary: Nineveh's city-wide repentance—from king to commoner, covering themselves in sackcloth and crying out to God—is presented as the sovereign fruit of God accomplishing His purposes through an unwilling servant.

context: Who can tell if God will turn and repent and turn away from his fierce anger that we perish not?
point_summary: Nineveh's king voiced uncertain but genuine hope that God might relent, and that corporate repentance—from throne to livestock—moved God to withhold the judgment He had declared.

context: And God saw their works. He saw their obedience.
point_summary: God's willingness to work through an unwilling servant—bringing Jonah to Nineveh despite his disobedience—resulted in citywide repentance that moved God to withhold the threatened judgment.

context: But it displeased Jonah exceedingly and he was very angry.
point_summary: Jonah's anger at Nineveh's repentance exposes a prophet who knew God's merciful character yet resented its extension to those he deemed unworthy of grace.

context: And he prayed unto the Lord and he said, I pray thee O Lord was not this my saying when I was yet of my country therefore I fled before in a tarshish. For I knew, look at this, for I knew that thou art a gracious God, a merciful, slow to anger, and a great kindness, and repentancy of the evil.
point_summary: Jonah's own confession in 4:2 reveals that his flight from God's call was rooted not in ignorance but in his certain knowledge that God would show mercy to Nineveh—making his disobedience a refusal of divine compassion, not a failure of faith.

context: Therefore now O Lord take I beseech thee my life from me for it is better for me to die than to live.
point_summary: Jonah's death wish in 4:3 exposes the depth of his merciless anger—he would rather die than witness God extend grace to a repentant Nineveh.

context: you're a Jonah
point_summary: Jonah 1:1 serves as the implicit backdrop for contrasting Isaiah's willing surrender to God's call with the pattern of avoidance and excuse-making that marks a "Jonah" who refuses to heed God's summons.

context: God says in the first chapter, in verse number 17, that he prepared a great fish to swallow Jonah after Jonah was cast overboard.
point_summary: God's preparation of the great fish to swallow Jonah—and the fish's subsequent obedience to spew him out—illustrates that inanimate creation obeys its Creator, implicitly rebuking Jonah's own disobedience.

context: you come to verse number 10, and the Bible says that God told the great fish, the whale, to spew him out.
point_summary: Even a great fish obeyed God's command instantly, making the point that inanimate creation submits to its Creator while Jonah—and by implication, God's people—so often do not.

context: Jonah chapter number four, verses one and two
point_summary: Jonah's anger and flight to Tarshish are revealed to stem not from ignorance of God's character but from full knowledge that God would show grace to Nineveh.

context: Jonah chapter number four, verses one and two
point_summary: Jonah's own confession in 4:2 reveals that his flight to Tarshish was driven not by ignorance of God's character but by foreknowledge of His grace toward Nineveh.

context: And it says this in verse number one, but it displeased Jonah exceedingly. And he was very angry.
point_summary: Jonah's anger at Nineveh's repentance, paired with his own confession of God's grace and mercy, introduces the sermon's central question of whether the God of the Old Testament is truly the same loving God revealed in the New.

context: Notice it says, he prayed unto the Lord and said, 'I pray thee, O Lord, was not this my saying 'when I was yet in my country? 'Therefore I fled before unto Tarshish, why? 'For I knew that thou art a gracious God, 'and merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness, 'and repentest thee of the evil.'
point_summary: Jonah's own confession in 4:2 — that God is gracious, merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness — serves as the sermon's launching point for answering whether the God of the Old Testament is the same loving God revealed in the New.

context: even as Jonah concluded there in Jonah 4
point_summary: Jonah's own confession in Jonah 4 that God is gracious serves as the sermon's governing conclusion: even when divine judgment falls on people groups, the character of God remains merciful.

context: There's a number of other things that are mentioned there in Jonah 4 and verse number two that we'll look at as we end this evening.
point_summary: Jonah's own confession in Jonah 4:2 that God is gracious serves as the sermon's governing conclusion, against which multiple Old Testament examples of divine judgment are measured to show that mercy and justice are not contradictory in God's character.

context: Jonah preaches this message after God got a hold of them in Jonah 3 and verse number 4 The Bible tells us that he began to enter into the city a day's journey and he cried and said yet 40 days in Nineveh shall be overthrown.
point_summary: Jonah's stark, unqualified proclamation of destruction in forty days establishes that Nineveh's judgment was certain and would have included even its most vulnerable inhabitants, had the city not repented.

context: Including the hundred and twenty thousand children in Nineveh and we get that number from Jonah 4 in verse number 11 After God deals with Jonah and the Gourd and all of that situation He says in verse 11 of chapter 4 and should not I spare Nineveh that great city where there are more than six
point_summary: God's stated reason for sparing Nineveh—over 120,000 persons unable to discern right from left—establishes the scale of innocent life that would have perished under judgment had Nineveh not repented.

context: ...based on the preaching of Jonah in verse number 4 of Jonah 3 that God would have destroyed Nineveh...
point_summary: Jonah's forty-day warning of Nineveh's overthrow was a genuine divine threat that would have destroyed the entire city, including its 120,000 children, apart from the people's repentant response.

context: ...Notice in verse number 5 of Jonah chapter number 3 the Bible says so the people of Nineveh believed God...
point_summary: Nineveh's collective repentance—believing God, fasting, and wearing sackcloth from greatest to least—stayed the divine judgment that would otherwise have destroyed the entire city, including its 120,000 children.

context: ...but in verse number 10 It says that God saw their works that they turned from their evil way...
point_summary: Nineveh's repentance — belief, fasting, and turning from evil — stayed God's declared judgment, demonstrating that the people's response directly determined whether divine destruction fell.

context: I said we'd end with Jonah 4.
point_summary: God's mercy toward Nineveh, which Jonah resented, is set against the backdrop of His unchanging character as both gracious and a consuming fire whose justice spans both Testaments.

context: it displeased Jonah exceedingly, and he is very angry
point_summary: Jonah's anger at Nineveh's sparing reveals that his flight to Tarshish was never about fear of failure but about knowing God's gracious character would override the judgment Jonah believed the wicked city deserved.

context: he prayed unto the Lord and said, I pray thee, O Lord, was not this my saying when I was yet in my country?
point_summary: Jonah's own confession in fleeing to Tarshish reveals that he knew God's character—gracious, merciful, slow to anger—and feared that knowledge would lead to Nineveh's sparing rather than its judgment.

context: Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city, and cry against it; for their wickedness is come up before me.
point_summary: God's awareness of Nineveh's wickedness—"their wickedness is come up before me"—parallels how divine knowledge of a city's sin precedes and motivates the prophetic call to speak against it.

context: Jonah began to enter into the city a day's journey, and he cried and said, Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown.
point_summary: God's declared judgment on Nineveh — "Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown" — was conditional, and the city's repentance stayed that destruction, demonstrating that divine warnings carry an implicit call to repent.

context: It displeased Jonah exceedingly, and he was very angry.
point_summary: Jonah's extreme displeasure at Nineveh's repentance reveals that he had fled his assignment precisely because he feared God would show mercy to Israel's hated enemies rather than destroy them.

context: They believe the message of God. They repented in sackcloth and ashes, I mean, from the greatest to the least, and it's amazing as you read chapter number 3, verses 5 through verse number 10.
point_summary: Nineveh's city-wide repentance in response to Jonah's preaching demonstrates that divine judgment can be stayed when a people turn to God, confirming the conditional nature of the threatened destruction.

context: They believe the message of God. They repented in sackcloth and ashes, I mean, from the greatest to the least, and it's amazing as you read chapter number 3, verses 5 through verse number 10.
point_summary: Nineveh's repentance staying God's announced judgment illustrates that divine warnings of destruction are conditional, grounded in God's character as gracious, merciful, and slow to anger.

context: Notice verse number 2. He Jonah prayed unto the Lord and said, I pray thee, O Lord, was not this my saying when I was yet in my country? Therefore I fled before unto Tarshish. Why? For I knew that thou art a gracious God, and merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness, and repentest thee of the evil.
point_summary: Jonah's own confession reveals that he fled precisely because he knew God's character—gracious, merciful, slow to anger—qualities that believers celebrate but that Jonah resented being extended to Nineveh.

context: For now, O Lord, take I beseech thee my life for me, for it is better for me to die than to live.
point_summary: Jonah's death wish in 4:3 illustrates that the very divine mercy believers treasure—grace, slowness to anger, readiness to pardon—was precisely what embittered him toward Nineveh.

context: The Lord had prepared it in verse number six, and then God prepared a vehement east wind and the gourd was no longer there.
point_summary: The gourd God prepared for Jonah serves as a foil to expose Jonah's misplaced pity—caring more for a plant than for the children and souls of Nineveh.

context: And notice what is said in verse number 10, then said the Lord, thou hast had pity on the gourd for the which thou hast not labored, neither made us that grow, which came up in a night and perished in a night.
point_summary: God's rebuke of Jonah contrasts the prophet's pity for a perishing gourd with God's compassion for 120,000 children in Nineveh, exposing the selfishness of withholding mercy from others.

context: And should not I spare Nineveh, that great city, wherein are more than six score thousand persons? that cannot discern between their right hand and their left hand.
point_summary: God's rebuke of Jonah's selective compassion — pitying a gourd but resenting mercy shown to 120,000 children and cattle — exposes the wrong response to divine grace extended toward others.

context: the message was simple, yet 40 days and Nineveh shall be destroyed
point_summary: Sackcloth in Nineveh illustrates that a simple prophetic warning of imminent destruction, received with genuine repentance, produces revival—the same solution Joel calls Israel toward.

context: Jonah obeyed better than he did right because how did Jonah obey he didn't do so good in Jonah chapter number one
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In Jonah chapter number three, we find that Jonah, the prophet of God, has now gotten right with God.
point_summary: Jonah 3 is introduced as the biblical model for the sermon's third point — that repentance is the required response when a nation stands under the announcement of divine judgment.

context: that the word of the Lord came unto Jonah the second time, saying
point_summary: God's unchanged commission to Jonah — delivered a second time after his repentance — illustrates that divine calls to preach judgment against wicked nations are not rescinded, only delayed until the messenger obeys.

context: Arise, go to Nineveh, and cry against it
point_summary: Jonah 1:2 establishes God's original command to confront Nineveh's wickedness—a command that remained unchanged even after Jonah's rebellion and restoration, underscoring that divine judgment on sin demands a prophetic response.

context: go into Nineveh, that great city, and preach unto it the preaching that I did thee
point_summary: God's unchanged command to Jonah—"preach unto it the preaching that I bid thee"—illustrates that divine calls to proclaim judgment do not shift, even when the messenger has resisted and required correction.

context: Chapter number one tells us all about his fleeing
point_summary: Jonah's initial flight from God's call in chapter one serves as backdrop to his transformed obedience in chapter three, illustrating that repentance restores a servant to his God-given mission.

context: The Bible tells us that Jonah arose and went unto Nineveh
point_summary: Jonah's urgent, single-day entry into a three-day city illustrates that once he obeyed God's unchanged call, he moved with maximum speed toward the task of preaching coming judgment.

context: now Nineveh was an exceeding great city of three days' journey
point_summary: Jonah's urgent, single-day entry into a three-day city illustrates that once a prophet is right with God, obedience to the divine commission becomes swift and wholehearted.

context: and Jonah began to enter into the city a day's journey
point_summary: Jonah's urgent, one-sentence proclamation—"Yet forty days and Nineveh shall be overthrown"—illustrates that God's judgment message is simple, direct, and demands immediate response.

context: these people in Nineveh, they believed God and they proclaimed a fast and they put on sackcloth from the greatest of them even to the least of them.
point_summary: Nineveh's repentance began with believing God—trusting that His announced judgment was true—and expressed itself in fasting and sackcloth from the greatest to the least.

context: she learned some things about Jonah and one of the things that she learned is that God is a God of second chances
point_summary: Jonah's story illustrates that God is a God of second chances, contrasting His persistent, restorative heart with the human tendency toward "one and done" judgment of offenders.

context: I'm sure that Jonah in his disobedience was certainly thankful that the great fish obeyed God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Who was the one that was obedient in the book of Jonah? Jonah or the whale?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown
context: Yet 40 days and Nineveh shall be destroyed
point_summary: Nineveh's repentance at Jonah's stark, simple warning illustrates that genuine response to God's word brings mercy, contrasted implicitly with Israel's rejection of the prophet's call.

context: Jonah got so angry with the Lord wanting to offer repentance and salvation or reformation to the Ninevites that Jonah ran away from the Lord
point_summary: Jonah's flight from God and subsequent pouting illustrate how deeply fretting over divine fairness—God blessing those we deem unworthy—can drive even an obedient prophet to anger and resentment.

context: go into Nineveh and preach 40 days and Nineveh shall be Destroyed
point_summary: God's announced judgment on Nineveh was genuine and certain, yet withheld when the city repented — illustrating that God's dealings with mankind are dependent upon man's actions.

context: you read the book of Jonah in the third chapter and you see how they Respond to the preaching the Word of God
point_summary: Nineveh's repentance in response to Jonah's preaching illustrates the sermon's governing principle that God's dealings with mankind are directly dependent upon man's actions.

context: I think about the book of Jonah. We won't take time to turn there, but I believe with all my heart that in 40 days God was going to destroy Nineveh
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jonah got in to the belly of that whale after he went in disobedience to God
point_summary: Jonah's rescue from the whale and subsequent preaching in Nineveh illustrates that God grants second opportunities to those who have been disobedient in their witness.

context: he got to Nineveh and he preached the gospel
point_summary: God's willingness to restore Jonah and send him again to Nineveh illustrates that he grants renewed opportunities to those who have failed in their gospel witness.
